content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Chain Prompting: Stepwise Fact Verification for Hallucination Reduction in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate factually incorrect statements, leading to hallucinations in their outputs. Current prompting techniques do not explicitly require LLMs to verify or justify their factual claims during generation.",
  "motivation": "Ensuring factual accuracy is critical for adoption of LLMs in high-stakes domains such as medicine, law, and education. Recent advances in chain-of-thought prompting improve reasoning, but do not directly mitigate hallucinations. Introducing a mechanism for explicit, stepwise fact verification within prompts could substantially improve factual reliability.",
  "method": "We propose Fact-Chain Prompting, a novel prompt structure that instructs the LLM to (1) decompose its answer into a sequence of atomic factual claims, (2) provide a justification or citation for each claim (from either internal knowledge or external retrieval), and (3) synthesize a final answer only if all claims are verified or justified. The prompt template is designed to guide LLM outputs into fact-verification chains by interleaving claims and supporting evidence. Optionally, retrieved documents can be injected into the prompt to improve claim justification.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate Fact-Chain Prompting on open-domain QA datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, Natural Questions) and fact-sensitive generation tasks (e.g., BioASQ). Baselines include standard direct prompting, chain-of-thought prompting, and retrieval-augmented generation. Hallucination rates and factual accuracy will be measured using human evaluation and automated fact-checking metrics (e.g., FEVER score, factual consistency classifiers)."
}
